title: Divide Pages

content.problemStatement:
Chef has NNN books such that the ithi^{th}ith book has AiA_iAi​ pages. He wants to divide **all** the books between Alice and Bob such that:


Both Alice and Bob get **at least** one book;
The number of pages allotted to Alice and Bob are either **both odd** or **both even**.
Find whether such distribution exists.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of books.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers, where the ithi^{th}ith integer denotes the number of pages in the ithi^{th}ith book.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, `YES`, if a valid distribution exists. Otherwise, print `NO`.


You can print each character in uppercase or lowercase. For example, `NO`, `no`, `No`, and `nO`, are all considered the same.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the number of books. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, where Ai denotes the number of pages in the i‑th book.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print YES if it is possible to give at least one book to Alice and at least one book to Bob such that the total pages each receives are both odd or both even; otherwise print NO. Each answer on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: total pages = 12 (even) and N>1 → YES. Test case 2: total pages = 7 (odd) → NO. Test case 3: total pages = 8 (even) and N>1 → YES.
First test case: total = 3 (odd) → NO. Second test case: total = 15 (odd) → NO.
Total pages = 8 (even) and both get at least one book, so a valid distribution exists.

solution.approach: The parity of the sum of all pages determines the answer. If there is only one book, the condition cannot be satisfied because one person would receive no book, so answer is NO. For N >= 2, we can always split the books so that both persons receive at least one book and the two sums have the same parity **iff** the total sum is even. When the total sum is even, we can give any single book to Alice and the rest to Bob; the parity of Alice's sum equals the parity of that single book, and Bob's sum = total - Alice's sum, which has the same parity because total is even. If the total sum is odd, the two sums would have opposite parity, violating the requirement. Hence answer is YES when N > 1 and total sum is even, otherwise NO.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case to compute the sum, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides input storage.

code.Java:
import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder out=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());while(T-- >0){int N=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());long sum=0;for(int i=0;i<N;i++){sum+=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());}if(N==1){out.append("NO
");}else if((sum & 1)==0){out.append("YES
");}else{out.append("NO
");}}System.out.print(out.toString());}}

        print(1)
